title: Te Whakapapa o te Hauora: The Living Legacy of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
subject: Health & Physical Education
culturalContext: Students will explore traditional Ngāti Kahungunu health practices through the lens of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (our local stream), connecting the mauri (life force) of the waterway to holistic wellbeing. The lesson draws on the pūrākau (legend) of Hinekorako, the water maiden who gifted healing knowledge to our ancestors along these banks.
learningObjectives: Identify and explain at least three traditional Māori health practices connected to water (rongoā, mirimiri, wai tapu) and their relevance to modern wellbeing
Create a detailed whakapapa map showing the connection between Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku's health and community wellbeing
Design and implement a practical water-quality monitoring activity using both mātauranga Māori and scientific approaches
Develop a personal hauora plan incorporating at least one traditional practice learned from local kaumātua

activities.title: Whakatau me te Whakapapa
activities.description: Begin with karakia at Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku. Students create visual whakapapa maps showing the connection between the stream's health (water quality, native species, riparian plants) and human wellbeing (physical, mental, spiritual health).

activities.title: Rongoā Wai Workshop
activities.description: Local kaumātua Aroha Whaitiri demonstrates traditional uses of water plants from our awa for healing (e.g., kawakawa for skin, pūhā for digestion). Students create their own simple rongoā preparations while learning proper protocols.

activities.title: Mauri Meter Investigation
activities.description: Students conduct paired water quality testing using both scientific measures (pH, clarity, temperature) and cultural indicators (presence of tuna/eels, clarity for wai tapu, riparian biodiversity). They compare datasets to assess the mauri of the stream.

activities.title: Waiata Hauora Composition
activities.description: In groups, students create original waiata or haka that reflect the connection between Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku and community wellbeing, incorporating specific health practices learned from kaumātua.

activities.title: Hauora Action Plan
activities.description: Students develop personal wellbeing plans that integrate one traditional water-based practice (e.g., morning mirimiri with cold water, preparing rongoā tea) and commit to one action to improve the health of our awa.

resources: Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku Water Testing Kit (pH strips, thermometers, clarity tubes)
'Rongoā Māori: A Practical Guide to Traditional Māori Medicine' by Rob McGowan
Ngāti Kahungunu Iwi Incorporated website - Te Hauora section

tasks: Completed whakapapa map showing connections between stream health and human wellbeing
Rongoā preparation journal with observations and reflections
Comparative water quality data analysis table
Performance of original waiata/haka demonstrating understanding
Personalised hauora action plan with implementation timeline

nzcAlignment: Health and Physical Education: A1 - Personal Health and Physical Development
Health and Physical Education: C1 - Relationships with Other People
Health and Physical Education: D1 - Healthy Communities and Environments
Science: Living World: Life Processes, Ecology
